12 Key Terms Regarding Pension Plans
Do you often worry about your post-retirement life? Do you sometimes feel you may outlive your savings and may not have anything substantial for your retirement years?
With the rising inflation rates, it may be natural to be bothered about such concerns, especially if you also have a family to take care of. An effective way to deal with such worries may be to opt for pension plans. What are pension plans? These are essentially life insurance products that may have a component of retirement pay-outs attached.
While life insurance coverage provides financial security to your loved ones in your absence, the regular pay-outs after you retire may allow you to live a happy and worry-free retired life. However, the world of pension plans may be a bit difficult to understand for a beginner. To help you out, we explore 12 key terms regarding pension plans to help demystify the world of retirement planning.
Sum assured
When you buy a pension plan, you may choose an amount your loved ones may receive if you passed away during the policy term. This amount may be referred to as the sum assured. You may pick a high sum assured for your loved ones to receive full financial support even in your absence.
Premium
The premium can be the amount you pay for the pension plan to provide you coverage and to keep it active throughout your tenure. The premium differs for each individual, based on subjective factors.
If you fail to pay the premium on time, your policy may become inactive after a certain period. Hence, the premium is one of the key terms in pension plans you must understand.
Nominee
Every life insurance policy has a nominee. The nominee is the individual whom you may appoint to receive the benefits of the life insurance policy after you may have passed away. It is the nominee who may raise a claim when required and receive compensation from the insurance company accordingly.
Accumulation stage
Usually, a pension plan may have two stages, the first of which may be called the accumulation stage. In this stage, the policyholder makes contributions (or pays the premiums) towards their pension plans. The duration of the accumulation stage may differ based on the kind of pension plan you may have opted for.
Vesting stage
The vesting stage may be the second stage and one of the key terms in a pension plan. It may usually begin once the policyholder retires or when they reach a certain age that may symbolise retirement. At this stage, the policyholder may start receiving pay-outs at the frequency they chose.
Deferred pension plan
It is a type of pension plan where the policyholder starts receiving pay-outs after a particular period has passed since the purchase of the plan. In a way, the accumulation stage of a deferred pension plan may be longer. Hence, one may be able to purchase a deferred pension plan at an early age as well.
Immediate pension plan
In this type of plan, the policyholder may start receiving the pay-outs soon after they purchase the plan and start paying the premium. For this reason, immediate pension plans may be more suitable for those nearing retirement.
Guaranteed returns
Policyholders looking for certainty with their retirement plans may find the guaranteed returns an appealing feature. With this feature, the policyholder may receive the total of all premiums paid at maturity or they may receive the fund value, whichever may be higher. Guaranteed returns may be an optional feature with most plans. You may want to read the policy wordings and/or get more clarity from your insurer
Return of purchase price
This lesser-known key term in pension plans may mean that, when the policyholder passes away, the purchase price may be returned to your nominees. The return of purchase price may also be an optional feature you may opt for by paying a small extra premium.
Joint Life Pension Plan
If you wish for your spouse to receive pension pay-outs even after you may have passed away, you may opt for the joint life option. If an unfortunate event were to lead to your demise, your spouse may receive 50% or 100% pay-outs which may help them live a financially secure life.
Premium paying term
The duration for which you pay the premiums into the pension plan may be referred to as the premium paying term. You may choose from the different premium paying terms  depending on the policy terms and conditions. Within your term, you may choose the frequency at which you plan to pay the premiums.
Due to the nature of an immediate pension plan, there may only be a single premium paying option available with it.
Payout frequency
When you opt for a pension plan, you have the option to choose the intervals at which you may receive the pension plan pay-outs. This is referred to as the payout frequency and may be one of the key terms regarding pension plans. Based on your selection, you may receive your pay-outs monthly, quarterly, half-yearly, or yearly.
These were some crucial terminologies associated with pension plans. While knowledge of these terms may help you get a better grasp of the concept, you should also know how to start with a retirement-cum-pension plan in the first place. Let us help you out.
How to Begin Retirement Planning
Here is a brief guide on how you may begin your retirement planning journey with pension plans:
Understand your needs
First, know what your retirement is going to be like and what your needs may be. Do you plan to have a leisurely retirement, involving relaxing in your home location and playing with your grandkids, or do you want an exciting travel-filled retired life? The finances for both these scenarios may differ. Other costs such as medical bills, daily expenses, and liabilities may have to be kept in mind too. Hence, you can outline your retirement needs and select options meeting your needs.
Set aside funds for retirement
Now that you may have an idea of your needs and how they may be funded, you need to figure out a way to support them via your present earnings. The amount you need to contribute towards a pension plan may also depend on your age. If you are young and retirement may still be a long way off, you may begin with relatively small amounts.
If you are late to retirement planning, it may be a better idea to park larger amounts in a pension plan. Also consider your present expenses, liabilities, inflation rates, other investment plans, and so on, when setting aside funds for retirement.
Review your plan regularly
It may be crucial for you to review your pension plan periodically to see if it is going in the direction you want it to. If that is not the case, you may make some modifications, such as opting for a top-up premium if available.
A pension plan may help you enjoy your retired life to a great extent by offering financial support to fulfil your needs in your golden years. Apart from funding daily expenses, a pension play may also take care of your other retirement goals, such as travelling or other hobbies. Hence, choose the correct plan and secure your golden years.
